title: The child with a sunburn rash, loose stools, and a wobbly gait
blurb: Dermatitis, diarrhea, and neurologic signs in a well-fed child — with a curious aminoaciduria that names the disease and explains the vitamin it mimics.

patient.complaint: A scaly, photosensitive rash on sun-exposed skin, intermittent diarrhea, and episodes of unsteadiness.

opening: A 6-year-old presents with a symmetric, scaly, hyperpigmented rash across the neck and dorsal hands/forearms that flares in sunlight, plus intermittent diarrhea and episodic cerebellar ataxia. His diet is actually adequate and varied. A prior sibling had similar skin findings that 'came and went.' Urine amino acid screen is abnormal. What do you want to ask, examine, or order next?
history: Well-nourished, no alcohol exposure, not on isoniazid or 5-FU. Symptoms are episodic and worsen with sun, fever, and poor intake. Consanguineous parents. No carcinoid features. Diet is not maize-heavy.
exam: Casal-necklace distribution dermatitis (sun-exposed, sharply demarcated). Intermittent wide-based ataxic gait during a flare. Cognition normal between episodes. No hepatomegaly, no flushing, no murmur.

prompts.diagnosis: What is the diagnosis, and what deficiency does it functionally mimic?
prompts.cause: What is defective, and how does it affect tryptophan?
prompts.mechanism: Trace how the defect produces a pellagra picture.

accepts: impaired tryptophan absorption in gut and kidney
less tryptophan absorbed so less niacin/nad synthesized

pinpoint.prompt: Click the amino acid whose defective absorption starves the de-novo niacin/NAD⁺ route.
pinpoint.correctMsg: Yes — tryptophan. In Hartnup disease a neutral amino-acid transporter (SLC6A19 / B⁰AT1) in the gut and proximal renal tubule fails, so neutral amino acids — most importantly tryptophan — are malabsorbed and spilled in the urine (the neutral aminoaciduria). Because tryptophan is the precursor for de-novo NAD⁺ (via kynurenine → quinolinate → niacin), less absorbed tryptophan means functional niacin deficiency → a pellagra picture (dermatitis, diarrhea, neurologic signs). Treat with a high-protein diet and niacin/nicotinamide.
pinpoint.wrongMsg: Not quite — look for the essential amino acid at the head of this pathway; its malabsorption is what limits niacin synthesis.
pinpoint.revealMsg: The answer was the Tryptophan node: the malabsorbed precursor of de-novo NAD⁺.

mcqs.stem: How does Hartnup pellagra differ from classic dietary (maize-based) pellagra, yet share a final pathway?
choices: Both are caused by too much niacin
Dietary pellagra = low niacin AND low tryptophan intake; Hartnup = adequate diet but malabsorbed tryptophan — both end in too little NAD⁺
Hartnup is caused by a niacin transporter defect
mcqs.expl: Corn is low in bioavailable niacin and in tryptophan, so a maize-heavy diet causes pellagra. Hartnup reaches the same NAD⁺ shortfall despite adequate diet because tryptophan can't be absorbed. The common endpoint is inadequate de-novo NAD⁺.

mcqs.stem: Which other drugs/conditions produce a pellagra-like state through the SAME tryptophan → niacin axis?
choices: Isoniazid (depletes B6 needed for the kynurenine pathway) and carcinoid (diverts tryptophan to serotonin)
mcqs.expl: Isoniazid depletes B6, a cofactor in the kynurenine → NAD⁺ route; carcinoid tumors shunt tryptophan into serotonin. Both leave too little tryptophan flux to NAD⁺ — a functional niacin deficiency by a different mechanism.

mcqs.stem: Why is Hartnup often intermittent and sometimes asymptomatic?
choices: The transporter works on alternate days
Adequate dietary protein and residual absorption usually supply enough tryptophan; symptoms surface under stress, fever, sun, or low intake
It resolves at puberty in all cases
mcqs.expl: Many with the transporter defect stay well because dietary protein and some residual/other-route absorption cover requirements. Deficiency becomes clinical when demand rises or intake falls — hence the episodic, stress-triggered course.
